title: --sudo option: 4-layer elevation architecture with pulse tracing
desc: How the --sudo flag triggers elevation across four independent layers: (1) MCP Launcher pre-execution gate, (2) Core startup CLI handler, (3) eye --sudo force-launch (non-destructive recovery), (4) Eye auto-launch inheritance. Pulse trail + reason string make UAC prompts traceable.
steps:
LAYER 1 — MCP Launcher gate (McpProxy.cs stdin router)
Pre-execution gate detects '"--sudo"' in incoming JSON-RPC lines.
If admin Core not running (_adminProc == IntPtr.Zero && !_adminMode):
  - Save request to _elevationRequestLine
  - Call existing SpawnAdminCore (unchanged)
  - Skip forwarding to normal Core
Existing reactive UIPI → _elevationRequired path untouched.

LAYER 2 — Core startup --sudo handler (Program.cs:233)
Runs when Core process starts with --sudo in its args.
Flow:
  1. Strip --sudo flag
  2. IsElevated() check — if already admin, no-op
  3. If not elevated:
     - eye command → force-launch path (layer 3)
     - other command → admin Eye proxy route (pipe-persistent)
       -> ElevatedEyeClient.IsAvailable() → ExecuteViaProxy
       -> UAC triggered only if admin Eye missing
PULSE steps: init 'core-sudo', line 'path=X', finish with result

LAYER 3 — eye --sudo force-launch (non-destructive)
Replaces old destructive kill-then-runas path.
Rationale: when Core sees --sudo without admin, Eye is likely abnormal.
Force-launch adds admin Eye side-by-side; new admin Eye cleans up old itself.
Code path:
  if (IsAvailable()) return 'already running'; exit 0
  else if (LaunchElevatedEye(reason)) return 'launched'; exit 0
  else return 'UAC cancelled'; exit 1
Old user Eye never killed by Core — responsibility delegated to new admin.

LAYER 4 — Eye auto-launch inheritance (AppBotEyeCommands.cs)
Guardian args now include '--sudo' when Program.IsElevated() is true.
Effect:
  - Admin Core respawning Eye → new Eye also admin + --sudo marker
  - User Core respawning Eye → regular user Eye (no --sudo)
Child process elevation is inherited from parent automatically;
--sudo just serves as the marker so new Eye routes through admin pipe.

PULSE TRACING — step-by-step debug via stderr
PulseStep.Init/Line/Finish calls scattered through Program.cs --sudo block.
All use force mode (bypass WKAPPBOT_PROFILE env var) so output always visible.
Format: [PULSE:core-sudo] 'label' +N ms (total=Nms)
Ring buffer (PulseStep.GetRecentTrail) retains last 20 lines for UAC prompts.

UAC REASON + TRAIL DUMP (ElevatedEyeProxy.cs)
LaunchElevatedEye(string reason) prints to stderr BEFORE UAC:
  [ELEVATION] ▼ Requesting admin rights: <reason>
  [ELEVATION] ── recent step trail ──
  [ELEVATION]   <last 5 pulse lines>
  [ELEVATION] ──────────────────────
Caller sites provide contextual reasons:
  eye force-launch → 'eye --sudo force-launch (Eye may be abnormal; recovery)'
  proxy route → '--sudo proxy route: command <cmd>'
  Readiness UIPI fallback → 'Readiness: UIPI-blocked target, proxy fallback'

DEBUGGING CHECKLIST
When --sudo behaves unexpectedly:
  1. Run 'wkappbot --sudo <cmd> 2>&1

grep PULSE' — see step-by-step flow
  2. Check trail output before UAC — 'ELEVATION ▼' reason tells you why
  3. Check admin Eye process: tasklist

grep wkappbot-core (expect 2+ cores)
  4. Check admin pipe: Test-Path '\.\pipe\wkappbot_elevated' (PowerShell)
  5. Regression tests: bin/wkappbot.hq/experience/tests/sudo/test-mcp-sudo-gate.sh
     Covers all 4 layers + pulse infrastructure, 9/9 tests.
author: claude
